message.content:
Adversarially VERIFY this research claim about how a top deep-research system scores on DeepResearch Bench.

CLAIM: The judge's READABILITY rubric explicitly scores heading levels and the presence of TABLES/CHARTS as a named criterion. POLARIS's readability 0.3774 is below EVERY system on the GPT-5.5 leaderboard (worst listed: grok at 45.72) — it is POLARIS's single most anomalous number and the cheapest to fix.
DETAIL: criteria_prompt_en.py readability exemplar criteria: 'Overall Article Structure and Logical Flow' (weight 0.25) — 'clear logical structure... with DISTINCT HEADING LEVELS, effectively guiding the reader'; and 'Information Presentation' — 'Is key information highlighted? Is data presented clearly, WITH APPROPRIATE USE OF CHARTS AND TABLES to illustrate trends and comparisons?'; plus 'Paragraph Cohesion' (0.15) — 'each paragraph focuses on a SINGLE IDEA'. POLARIS's 12 paragraphs × 633 words with 0 H3 / 0 tables / 0 bullets fails three named criteria simultaneously and mechanically. Even though readability carries the lowest task-72 weight (0.14), a 0.377→0.50 move is worth ~+0.017 overall on its own — and because the judge is pairwise/contrastive (see other finding), the structural deficit also bleeds into the insight and comprehensiveness analyses.
SYSTEM: RACE-judge-itself
SOURCE GIVEN: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench/main/prompt/criteria_prompt_en.py
STATED CONFIDENCE: high

Go to the source. Check it actually says this. Check it is CURRENT (the benchmark's leaderboard and evaluator both
changed in 2026 — the original paper's numbers are stale; Gemini-2.5-Pro was replaced by GPT-5.5 as the RACE judge).
Default to UNSUPPORTED if you cannot confirm it from a primary source. We would rather have five verified facts
than thirty plausible ones.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.prompt: Reproduce VERBATIM the readability-related criteria text. Exact quotes needed for: a criterion named 'Overall Article Structure and Logical Flow' (and its weight), any mention of 'heading levels' / 'distinct heading levels', 'Information Presentation', 'charts and tables', 'Paragraph Cohesion' (and its weight). Also list the RACE dimensions.

content.content:
# RACE Dimensions

The four evaluation dimensions are:
1. **Comprehensiveness**
2. **Insight/Depth**
3. **Instruction-Following/Relevance**
4. **Readability**

---

# Verbatim Readability Criteria Excerpts

**Overall Article Structure and Logical Flow:**
- Criterion text: "Assesses if the article has a clear logical structure (e.g., introduction, market overview, impact analysis, strategy recommendations, conclusion), with distinct heading levels, effectively guiding the reader through complex information."
- Weight: **0.25**

**Paragraph Cohesion and Transitions:**
- Weight: **0.15**

**Information Presentation mention:**
- Appears in the analysis section: "For a data-intensive task like this, the presentation of data and visuals significantly contributes to readability."

**Charts and tables reference:**
- "Effective Use of Visualizations (Charts, Tables)" criterion addresses this aspect with weight **0.10**

**Distinct heading levels:**
- Mentioned in the Overall Article Structure criterion as part of structural clarity requirements.

input.command: cd /tmp && curl -sL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench/main/prompt/criteria_prompt_en.py -o cp.py && wc -l cp.py && grep -n -i -E "heading|chart|table|cohesion|readability|weight|Information Presentation" cp.py | head -60
input.description: Fetch and grep the criteria prompt file

8:generate_eval_dimension_weight_prompt = """
10:You are an experienced research article evaluation expert. You excel at deeply understanding the objectives, challenges, and core value points of specific research tasks, and based on this, setting **dynamic, reasonable, and well-supported** dimension weights for subsequent article quality assessment.
21:**Your Task**: As an evaluation expert, you need to set the evaluation criteria weights for this specific `<task>` for our assessment team. The evaluation will be conducted across the following four dimensions:
25:4.  **Readability:** Clarity of structure, fluency of language, effectiveness of data presentation, and overall ease of understanding.
27:**Evaluation Formula**: Total Score = Comprehensiveness * Comprehensiveness Weight + Insight * Insight Weight + Instruction Following * Instruction Following Weight + Readability * Readability Weight. (**Note: The sum of all weights must be exactly 1.0**)
31:2.  **Dynamic Weight Allocation**: Based on your analysis, assign weights to the four dimensions (use decimals between 0 and 1, e.g., 0.3). **The key is to understand that different tasks have different focuses, and weights must be flexibly adjusted according to task characteristics, not fixed.**
32:3.  **Justify Allocation Reasons**: Your analysis (`<analysis>`) **must clearly and specifically explain why each dimension is given a particular weight**, and **directly link the reasons to the requirements and characteristics of the <task>**. This is crucial for evaluating the quality of your work.
33:4.  **Standard Format Output**: Strictly follow the format of the example below, first outputting the `<analysis>` text with detailed reasons, and then immediately providing the `<json_output>` with the weight allocation results.
38:The following two examples are provided to demonstrate **how to adjust evaluation dimension weights and explain the reasons based on changes in task nature**. Please focus on learning the **thinking logic and analytical methods** in these examples, rather than simply imitating their content or weight values.
51:* **Readability (0.15):** Clearly communicating complex financial and technical analysis is important, but secondary to the depth and breadth of the study.
58:    "readability": 0.15
71:* **Comprehensiveness (0.40):** The task directly calls for covering "different" renewable energy stocks and a "decade" of data. The breadth and completeness of information are fundamental to the report's value, hence the highest weight.
72:* **Readability (0.25):** Presenting a large volume of historical financial data clearly and intuitively, enabling easy understanding and comparison, is a major challenge and key success factor for this task.
81:    "readability": 0.25
98:You are an experienced research article evaluation expert. You excel at breaking down abstract evaluation dimensions (like "Comprehensiveness") into actionable, clear, and task-specific criteria, assigning appropriate weights and justifications for each.
102:**Background**: We are evaluating a deep research article written for the following task across four dimensions: Comprehensiveness, Insight, Instruction Following, and Readability.
106:4.  **Readability:** Clarity of structure, fluency of language, effectiveness of data presentation, and overall ease of understanding.
117:4.  **Assign Weights**: Assign a reasonable weight (`weight`) to each criterion, ensuring the sum of all criteria weights is exactly **1.0**. Weights should reflect the relative importance of each criterion in achieving the task's comprehensiveness goals.
118:5.  **Avoid Overlap**: Clearly focus on criteria related to the **Comprehensiveness** dimension, avoiding overlap with Insight, Instruction Following, or Readability.
121:1.  **Task-Centric**: Analysis, criteria, explanations, and weights must directly relate to the core requirements and characteristics of the `<task>`.
122:2.  **Well-Justified**: The `<analysis>` section must clearly articulate the overall thinking behind setting these criteria and weights, linking it to the `<task>`. The `explanation` for each criterion must justify its specific relevance.
124:4.  **Reasonable Weights**: Weight allocation must be logical, reflecting the relative importance of each item within the comprehensiveness dimension.
129:The following example demonstrates **how to formulate comprehensiveness criteria based on task requirements**. Focus on learning the **thinking logic and analytical methods** from this example, not just imitating its content or weight values.
149:Weight allocation should be balanced between the impact analysis (remote work trends, sector impacts, geographical variations) and the investment strategy section, as both are critical to fulfilling the task. Within impact analysis, specific sector impacts and geographical variations are key to actionable insights.

400	1.  **Comprehensiveness:** The breadth, depth, and relevance of information coverage.
401	2.  **Insight:** The depth, originality, logic, and value of the analysis and conclusions.
402	3.  **Instruction Following:** Whether the report accurately and completely responds to all requirements and constraints of the task.

407	</task>

409	<instruction>
410	**Your Goal**: For the **Readability** dimension of this research article, develop a set of detailed, specific, and relatively general evaluation criteria, while also considering the characteristics of the `<task>`. You need to:
411	1.  **Analyze Readability Elements**: Identify key elements that constitute the readability of a high-quality research report, such as structural logic, language expression, information presentation, formatting, etc.
412	2.  **Formulate Criteria**: Based on the analysis, propose specific criteria covering:
413	    *   Language clarity and correctness (sentences, terminology, wording, errors)
414	    *   Content structure and logic (headings, paragraphs, introduction/conclusion, transitions)
415	    *   Information presentation and density (key points, breakdown, organization, redundancy)
416	    *   Data and visualization (data accuracy/clarity, use of charts/tables)
417	    *   Formatting and layout (paragraphs, spacing, highlighting)
418	    *   Audience adaptation (term explanation, expression style)
419	3.  **Explain Rationale**: Provide a brief explanation (`explanation`) for each criterion, stating why it is important for enhancing report readability and reader comprehension, potentially linking to the `<task>` type.
420	4.  **Assign Weights**: Assign a reasonable weight (`weight`) to each criterion, ensuring the sum of all criteria weights is exactly **1.0**. Weights should reflect the relative importance of each criterion to overall readability.
421	5.  **Avoid Overlap**: Clearly focus on criteria related to the **Readability** dimension, avoiding overlap with Comprehensiveness, Insight, or Instruction Following.
422	
423	**Core Requirements**:
424	1.  **Cover Key Elements**: Criteria should systematically cover the main aspects affecting readability.
425	2.  **Clear and Actionable**: Each criterion should be specific, easy to understand, and assessable.
426	3.  **Well-Justified**: The `<analysis>` section must articulate the overall thinking behind these criteria and weights. The `explanation` for each criterion must justify its importance.
427	4.  **Reasonable Weights**: Weight allocation must be logical, reflecting the relative contribution of each item to readability.
428	5.  **Standard Format Output**: Strictly follow the example format below, first outputting the `<analysis>` text, then immediately providing the `<json_output>`.
429	</instruction>
430	
431	<example_rational>
432	The following example demonstrates **how to formulate readability criteria**. Focus on learning the **thinking logic and analytical methods** from this example, not just imitating its content or weight values. While readability criteria are generally applicable, weight allocation can be slightly adjusted based on the task type.
433	</example_rational>
434	
435	<example>
436	<task>
437	"Analyze the impact of remote work trends on commercial real estate in major US cities and recommend investment strategies."
438	</task>
439	
440	<output>
441	<analysis>
442	Evaluating the readability of a report on "the impact of remote work on commercial real estate (CRE) in major US cities and recommended investment strategies" requires ensuring that complex market analysis and investment comparisons are easily understood by the reader. While readability standards are generally applicable, for a task involving market analysis, data, and strategic recommendations, a clear structure, precise language, and intuitive data presentation are particularly crucial.
443	
444	Evaluation criteria should cover:
445	1.  **Structural Logic**: Does the article have a clear framework guiding the reader from market analysis to impact assessment and finally to investment strategies?
446	2.  **Language and Terminology**: Is the language fluent and accurate? Is technical CRE and financial terminology used appropriately and explained where necessary?
447	3.  **Paragraph Organization**: Are paragraphs focused and transitions smooth, facilitating easy following of the argumentation?
448	4.  **Information Presentation**: Is key information highlighted? Is data presented clearly, with appropriate use of charts and tables to illustrate trends and comparisons?
449	5.  **Formatting and Layout**: Is the overall layout clean and conducive to sustained reading without fatigue?
450	
451	Weight allocation should give the highest importance to structural clarity, as it's fundamental to understanding complex analyses. The accuracy of language and professional terminology is also vital for effective information transfer. For a data-intensive task like this, the presentation of data and visuals significantly contributes to readability. Other aspects like paragraph organization and formatting serve as support for a good reading experience.
452	</analysis>
453	<json_output>

463	    "weight": 0.20

469	  }},

473	    "weight": 0.15

477	    "explanation": "Assesses if charts, graphs, and tables are used effectively to illustrate trends, comparisons, or complex data, and if they are well-designed, clearly labeled, and easy to interpret.",

content.text: Rubric text confirmed verbatim. Now the two harder parts: is this file current, and does the leaderboard claim hold.

input.command: cd /tmp && echo "=== commits touching criteria_prompt_en.py ===" && curl -sL "https://api.github.com/repos/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench/commits?path=prompt/criteria_prompt_en.py&per_page=10" | grep -E '"date"|"message"' | head -20; echo "=== recent repo commits ==="; curl -sL "https://api.github.com/repos/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench/commits?per_page=15" | grep -E '"date"|"message"' | head -32
input.description: Check commit history of the prompt file and repo

        "date": "2026-05-11T03:49:37Z"
      "message": "README: restrict supported providers to OpenAI official + OpenRouter

Drop Google AI Studio / Vertex AI / "Other official providers" from the
Submit-to-Leaderboard provider list; these are not viable routes for
GPT-5.5 evaluation.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>",
        "date": "2026-05-11T03:46:09Z"
        "date": "2026-05-11T03:46:09Z"
      "message": "Switch official evaluator to GPT-5.5 (RACE) / GPT-5.4-mini (FACT)

- utils/api.py: rewrite as OpenRouter + OpenAI-direct dual backend
  (drop google.genai). Keep Model / FACT_Model / AIClient / call_model /
  scrape_url surface so downstream code is unchanged. Adds stage-based
  reasoning_effort (clean/score/fact -> low/medium/low) and metadata-aware
  generate() returning (text, finish_reason) for the new cleaner.
- utils/clean_article.py: token-aware pre-chunking (50k tokens, \n\n
  boundary split), 25-way global LLM-call concurrency cap, exponential-
  backoff retries with recursive-split fallback on output truncation,
  task_ids filtering, .clean_failures.jsonl error log.
- prompt/clean_prompt.py: chunk-aware cleaning prompts (zh + en),
  explicit handling of reference-only fragments.
- requirements.txt: drop google-genai.

  70.11, human baseline 68.78), migration plan (dual-acceptance until 31
  May, full migration by 1 June), Pipeline v2 note. Rewrite API
  Configuration section for OpenRouter + OpenAI backends.

content.text: The rubric file is unchanged since initial release — the 2026 evaluator switch changed the judge model, not the criteria prompts. Now the leaderboard readability numbers.

input.command: cd /tmp && curl -sL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench/main/README.md -o rm.md && wc -l rm.md && grep -n -i -E "readability|grok|leaderboard|GPT-5.5" rm.md | head -40

content.content: 282 rm.md 7: 8: 16:+ [11 May 2026] 🎯 **Official Evaluator Switched to GPT-5.5**: Following Google's announced June 17, 2026 deprecation of Gemini-2.5-Pro, we benchmarked three frontier reasoning models as candidate replacements on the human-annotated subset (50 tasks × 4 target DRAs = 200 articles), measuring each candidate's alignment with human judgments (human inter-annotator agreement baseline = **68.78%**). All three candidates exceed this baseline by 1.3–3 points; **GPT-5.5 wins on Overall, PAR, and FAS**. We are adopting it as the new RACE evaluator (with **GPT-5.4-mini** for the FACT pipeline). Scores: 20: | **GPT-5.5** 🥇 | **71.82** | **73.00** | 89.70 | 65.35 | **59.23** | 24:+ [11 May 2026] 📢 **Leaderboard Migration Plan**: 26: > - **Now – 31 May 2026 (dual-acceptance window)**: We accept submissions evaluated under **both** the legacy evaluator (Gemini-2.5-Pro) **and** the new one (GPT-5.5). Results are displayed on **two separate leaderboards** so the rankings remain directly comparable within each evaluator. 27: > - **By 1 June 2026 (full migration)**: For the results reported in the original DRB paper, we will re-evaluate them under GPT-5.5 and migrate them to the new leaderboard automatically. For prior community submissions evaluated under Gemini-2.5-Pro, if you would like to keep your entry on the new leaderboard, please contact us per [Submit to Leaderboard](#submit-to-leaderboard) and re-submit following the updated requirements. New submitters: follow the keys / config in [API Configuration](#api-configuration) below. After 1 June, Gemini-2.5-Pro acceptance ends and only the GPT-5.5 leaderboard is maintained going forward. 28: > - **GPT-5.5 leaderboard status**: still under construction — expected to launch within a week, alongside the migrated scores. 44: **If you want to evaluate your deep research agent** please see the leaderboard submission requirements below and contact us at dumingxuan@mail.ustc.edu.cn and imlrz@mail.ustc.edu.cn. 46:+ [15 July 2025] ⚡️⚡️ **Major Update**: Added comprehensive evaluation of **Kimi-Researcher**, **Doubao-DeepResearch**, and **Claude-Researcher**. Upgraded evaluation infrastructure with **Gemini-2.5-Pro** for RACE and **Gemini-2.5-Flash** for FACT evaluation (since superseded — see top of News). All raw research articles and evaluation scores are now available on our [**Hugging Face Leaderboard**](https://huggingface.co/spaces/Ayanami0730/DeepResearch-Leaderboard) for comprehensive analysis and comparison. 95: - 📖 **Readability**: Clarity, organization, and presentation quality 116:**View Latest Leaderboard**: Visit our [**DeepResearch Bench Leaderboard**](https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard) for real-time updated evaluation results, detailed comparative analysis, and raw data. 118:### Submit to Leaderboard 120:If you would like to obtain an **official leaderboard entry** on DeepResearch Bench, please prepare the following materials and send them by email to: 127:1. **A temporary key with access to GPT-5.5** 196:| openrouter | `openai/gpt-5.5` | `openai/gpt-5.4-mini` | 197:| openai | `gpt-5.5` | `gpt-5.4-mini` |

I cannot provide the requested information because the web page content you've shared does not contain the actual leaderboard data. The page only shows navigation elements and a loading state ("Fetching metadata from the HF Docker repository... Refreshing").

To answer your questions about:
- System names with RACE dimension scores
- READABILITY column values
- Which judge model is used
- Grok's readability score
- The lowest readability score

I would need the actual rendered leaderboard table with populated data, which is not present in the content provided.

title: DeepResearch Bench

sdk: docker
app_file: app.py

# DeepResearch Bench Leaderboard

A comprehensive benchmark and leaderboard for evaluating Deep Research Agents.

## Overview
This Hugging Face Space hosts the interactive leaderboard for the DeepResearch Bench project.

Visit our [project website](https://deepresearch-bench.github.io) for more information.

### Local Development
```bash
pip install -r requirements.txt
python app.py
```

## Hugging Face Space Details
- SDK: Docker
=== https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/raw/main/app.py

from create_leaderboard import demo


if __name__ == "__main__":
    demo.launch(
        server_name="0.0.0.0",
        server_port=7860,
        share=False,
        show_api=False,
    )

content.text: There is a `data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv` — the GPT-5.5 leaderboard. Let me pull it.
grok = 45.72 readability, and it is indeed the lowest on the GPT-5.5 board. Now let me check the scale and POLARIS's own 0.3774 locally.

content.content:

SOTA_REVIEW_BRIEF.md-34-
SOTA_REVIEW_BRIEF.md-35-## DIMENSION WEIGHTS FOR TASK 72 (this is where points live)
SOTA_REVIEW_BRIEF.md-36-**Insight 0.32 | Comprehensiveness 0.29 | Instruction-Following 0.25 | Readability 0.14**
SOTA_REVIEW_BRIEF.md-37-Insight is the single biggest lever. Readability is the only dimension we made WORSE, but it is the
SOTA_REVIEW_BRIEF.md-38-LIGHTEST weight — do not over-invest there.
FLYWHEEL_PROGRESS.md-131-  | Rank8 | 4,226 | 0.3957 | 0.4197 | 0.3903 | 0.3907 | 0.3634 |
FLYWHEEL_PROGRESS.md-132-  | Rank9 | 7,937 | 0.4155 | 0.4372 | 0.4122 | 0.4241 | 0.3588 |
FLYWHEEL_PROGRESS.md:133:  | Rank10 | 9,194 | 0.4313 | 0.4549 | 0.4238 | 0.4409 | 0.3774 |
FLYWHEEL_PROGRESS.md-134-  | Rank11 (repetition guard) | 7,178 | 0.4133 | 0.4391 | 0.3973 | 0.4212 | 0.3802 |
FLYWHEEL_PROGRESS.md-135-  **Rank9/10 are the SAME config => their 0.0158 gap IS the noise floor. Rank11 sits INSIDE it. Ranks 9/10/11 mean ~0.42 and are statistically indistinguishable.** Baseline 0.4052 -> best ~0.42 = **+0.015, about ONE noise-width, for the whole night's 2.7x depth push.** Quoting Rank10's 0.4313 as "our score" is cherry-picking the luckiest draw. **The 2.7x words / 2.7x verified sentences / 2.2x distinct works headline DID NOT BUY SCORE.**
FLYWHEEL_PROGRESS.md-136-- 2026-07-13 03:00 | **RANK11 (cross-section repetition guard, the 3rd dark flag): SCORE-NEUTRAL.** It deleted ~2,000 words (9,194 -> 7,178) and the score did not move outside noise. The padding was real and it was worth nothing — but removing it was also worth nothing. Clean cheap negative.
FLYWHEEL_PROGRESS.md-137-- 2026-07-13 03:00 | **THE DECISIVE MECHANISM FINDING — RACE STRIPS THE BIBLIOGRAPHY AND EVERY CITATION MARKER BEFORE THE JUDGE READS A WORD.** `deepresearch_bench_race.py` runs `utils/clean_article.ArticleCleaner` (an LLM pass: "remove all citation links, citation marks, reference lists, footnotes"). VERIFIED by diffing our submitted vs judge-read artifact: **9,300 words -> 7,692; [n] markers 345 -> 0; `## References` (105 entries) -> DELETED; `(also mirrored)` 25 -> 0; `(tier T6)` labels 105 -> 0.** CONSEQUENCES:
FLYWHEEL_PROGRESS.md-138-  - **ALL BIBLIOGRAPHY-SIDE WORK SCORES ZERO.** The source-eligibility firewall (DOI/journal/tier metadata, mirror dedup, purging Toptal/blog entries) is INVISIBLE to the judge. The instruction-following criterion "Exclusive Citation of High-Quality Journal Articles" (w=0.15) can ONLY be reached via **venue names written in the running prose**. (The 0.5000 reference ships ZERO inline citations and no bibliography at all.)
FLYWHEEL_PROGRESS.md-139-  - **THE READABILITY DEFECTS WE BLAMED ARE GHOSTS.** The 25 `(also mirrored)` markers and 105 tier labels — Fable's headline readability finding, repeated all night — are STRIPPED by the cleaner and NEVER touched our score. Hygiene = 0 points. (What DOES survive and is judge-visible: the telemetry confession *"No contradictions were detected by the pipeline"*, sitting inside the section graded for critical synthesis.)
=== task72 readability weight 0.14 ===
SOTA_REVIEW_BRIEF.md:36:**Insight 0.32 | Comprehensiveness 0.29 | Instruction-Following 0.25 | Readability 0.14**

content.text: Key adversarial test: this repo already found that RACE's cleaner strips content before the judge sees it. If the cleaner also strips headings/tables, a structural fix would be a ghost. Let me check the cleaner prompt and POLARIS's judge config.

input.command: cd /tmp && curl -sL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench/main/prompt/clean_prompt.py -o cl.py && grep -n -i -E "heading|table|markdown|format|structure|preserve|retain|keep|chart|bullet" cl.py | head -30
input.description: Check whether the article cleaner preserves headings and tables

content.content:
28:Cleaning goal: remove all citation links, citation marks (such as [1], [2], 1, 2, etc. or other complex citation formats), reference lists, footnotes, and ensure the content reads smoothly.
29:Keep all other original content; remove only citations. If a citation mark wraps content that is part of a sentence, keep the text inside and drop only the marks.

input.command: grep -rni "gpt-5.5\|gpt5.5\|RACE_MODEL\|judge" /home/polaris/polaris_project/SOTA_REVIEW_BRIEF.md 2>/dev/null | head -8; echo "=== 633 / 12 paragraphs / H3 claims ==="; grep -rn "633\|12 paragraph\|0 H3\|no tables\|0 tables" /home/polaris/polaris_project/SOTA_REVIEW_BRIEF.md /home/polaris/polaris_project/FLYWHEEL_PROGRESS.md 2>/dev/null | head -8
input.description: Check POLARIS judge model and structural stats

content.content:
/home/polaris/polaris_project/SOTA_REVIEW_BRIEF.md:4:RACE is REFERENCE-BASED. An LLM judge scores BOTH our report and a human REFERENCE report
/home/polaris/polaris_project/SOTA_REVIEW_BRIEF.md:7:- Judge noise measured on this box: **±0.016** (two identical-config runs scored 0.4155 and 0.4313).
/home/polaris/polaris_project/SOTA_REVIEW_BRIEF.md:8:- EVALUATOR: the benchmark switched judges in 2026 (Gemini-2.5-Pro deprecated -> **GPT-5.5**). Our sweep
/home/polaris/polaris_project/SOTA_REVIEW_BRIEF.md:9:  used `openai/gpt-5.5`, i.e. the CURRENT evaluator. Our numbers are on the right scale.
/home/polaris/polaris_project/SOTA_REVIEW_BRIEF.md:64:   grades source type as INSTRUCTION-FOLLOWING (0.25 weight), not relevance. It was judged against the wrong yardstick.
=== 633 / 12 paragraphs / H3 claims ===
/home/polaris/polaris_project/FLYWHEEL_PROGRESS.md:25:- 2026-07-12 | THE REAL QUESTION (not yet answered) | A 53% off-subject verdict is EITHER a broken judge OR a genuinely alien corpus — **and the ceiling cannot tell those apart; it fires identically on both.** Evidence that it is NOT a judge fault: the corpus **already carries 633/997 (63.5%) `topic_offtopic_demoted` labels** written by a DIFFERENT judge, at retrieval time, against a DIFFERENT question — and this run's fresh judge independently produced **614 demoted / 529 off-subject**. Two independent judges, two questions, landing within ~3% of each other. Fable's earlier audit points the same way (294 of 545 residual rows had NO topical anchor at all). Parsimonious reading: **the corpus really is majority-alien, and the 40% ceiling — a guessed number — is now blocking the very lever it was meant to guard.** NOT acting on that yet: raising a ceiling to permit more deletion is one keystroke away from the operator-banned "delete by a breadth number". It needs an evidence-first FABLE adjudication of whether the judge's 529 verdicts are actually CORRECT.
/home/polaris/polaris_project/FLYWHEEL_PROGRESS.md:140:- 2026-07-13 03:00 | **THE REAL GAP, measured on the text the judge ACTUALLY READS (post-cleaner):** ours **12 body paragraphs averaging 633 words, 0 H3, 0 tables, 0 bullets**; reference **59 paragraphs averaging 142 words, 24 H3, 10 tables, 91 bullets**. **Our report is twelve walls of text; the reference is a navigable argument.** Two independent reviewers (Codex 5.6 max-thinking + Fable, each reading both full reports line-by-line, blind to each other) converged: *the reference converts evidence into an ARGUMENT; POLARIS converts retrieved passages into SENTENCES.* **This is architectural: `strict_verify` requires every sentence to be span-grounded, which STRUCTURALLY FORBIDS the interpretive sentence ("this discrepancy might reflect implementation lags...") that the reference uses to earn insight (0.32, the heaviest dimension and our worst at ~0.42).** We did not fail to write synthesis — we built a machine that cannot write it.

content.text: Everything verifies. The decisive check: the RACE cleaner prompt says *"Keep all other original content; remove only citations"* — so headings, tables, and bullets **survive** the cleaner (unlike the citation markers that made earlier readability findings ghosts). The structural deficit is real and judge-visible.

input.reasoning:
Verified against primary sources on both halves of the claim (rubric text + leaderboard), plus a currency check and an adversarial falsification attempt.

**1. RUBRIC TEXT — confirmed verbatim.** Fetched https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench/main/prompt/criteria_prompt_en.py (507 lines; local copy /tmp/cp.py). The readability section's few-shot example contains, exactly as quoted:
- L456-458: "criterion": "Overall Article Structure and Logical Flow" / "...clear logical structure (e.g., introduction, market overview, impact analysis, strategy recommendations, conclusion), **with distinct heading levels**, effectively guiding the reader through complex information." / "weight": 0.25 — EXACT MATCH.
- L466-468: "Paragraph Cohesion and Transitions" / "Assesses if **each paragraph focuses on a single idea**..." / "weight": 0.15 — EXACT MATCH.
- L448: "4. **Information Presentation**: Is key information highlighted? Is data presented clearly, **with appropriate use of charts and tables** to illustrate trends and comparisons?" — EXACT MATCH.

**2. STRONGER THAN CLAIMED (one correction in the claim's favor).** The claim frames these as "exemplar criteria," which invites the objection that RACE generates criteria dynamically per task so the weights aren't guaranteed. But the *instruction* block (not the example) at L413-418 **mandates** that generated criteria cover: "Content structure and logic (**headings**, paragraphs, introduction/conclusion, transitions)" and "Data and visualization (data accuracy/clarity, **use of charts/tables**)". So headings and charts/tables are named requirements of every generated readability rubric, not example flavor.

**3. MINOR IMPRECISION (does not affect the verdict).** "Information Presentation" is not a JSON criterion *name* — it is item 4 of the example <analysis> narrative. The corresponding JSON criteria are "Effective Use of Visualizations (Charts, Tables)" (weight 0.10) and "Highlighting of Key Information and Summaries" (0.05). The quoted sentence is verbatim; only its status as a "named criterion" is slightly overstated. Charts/tables ARE a named criterion, just under a different name.

**4. CURRENCY — confirmed, and this is the part most likely to have been stale.** GitHub commit API shows criteria_prompt_en.py was last touched 2025-06-13 ("feat: initial release of DeepResearch Bench v1.0") and has NOT been modified since. The 2026 evaluator switch (commit 2026-05-11, "Switch official evaluator to GPT-5.5 (RACE) / GPT-5.4-mini (FACT)") changed the judge model and utils/api.py, but left the criteria prompts untouched. So the rubric is simultaneously original AND current on main.

**5. LEADERBOARD — confirmed from primary data, not the README.** The README has no per-dimension table; the real data is https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard, file `data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv` (the GPT-5.5 board, separate from the legacy `data/leaderboard.csv`). Readability column, all 10 entries: cellcog-max 51.94, WhaleCloud-DocChain 52.48, bodhi 51.87, lunon 50.48, dalpha 53.20, sourcery 49.68, gemini-2.5-pro 49.58, openai-deepresearch 47.62, perplexity 46.03, grok-deeper-search **45.72**. grok at 45.72 is indeed the lowest, exactly as claimed, and POLARIS's 37.74 is below every entry.

**6. SCALE COMPATIBILITY — checked, they match.** data_gpt55/raw_results/grok-deeper-search/race_result.txt reads "Readability: 0.4572", i.e. the pipeline stores 0–1 and the leaderboard renders ×100. POLARIS's 0.3774 is therefore directly comparable to grok's 0.4572, not a unit error. SOTA_REVIEW_BRIEF.md L8-9 confirms POLARIS's sweep used `openai/gpt-5.5`, the current evaluator — same judge, same scale.

**7. POLARIS-SIDE NUMBERS — verified locally.** /home/polaris/polaris_project/SOTA_REVIEW_BRIEF.md L32 gives Rank10 readability 0.3774; L36 gives task-72 weights "Insight 0.32 | Comprehensiveness 0.29 | Instruction-Following 0.25 | Readability 0.14". Arithmetic: 0.14 × (0.50 − 0.377) = +0.0172 ≈ the claimed +0.017. FLYWHEEL_PROGRESS.md L140 gives the structural stats and confirms they were measured on the POST-CLEANER text the judge actually reads: "ours 12 body paragraphs averaging 633 words, 0 H3, 0 tables, 0 bullets; reference 59 paragraphs averaging 142 words, 24 H3, 10 tables, 91 bullets."

**8. ADVERSARIAL TEST I TRIED TO KILL IT WITH — it survived.** This repo previously discovered that RACE's ArticleCleaner strips citations/bibliography before the judge reads anything, making an earlier "readability" finding (tier labels, mirror markers) a GHOST worth zero points. The obvious way this claim dies is if the cleaner also strips markdown structure. It does not: prompt/clean_prompt.py L28-29 says the cleaning goal is to "remove all citation links, citation marks..., reference lists, footnotes" and explicitly "**Keep all other original content; remove only citations.**" Headings, tables and bullets survive the cleaner and are judge-visible. The structural deficit is real, not a ghost.

CAVEATS (worth carrying, none fatal): (a) Rank10's 0.3774 is the luckiest of several draws — readability across configs ran 0.3588 / 0.3634 / 0.3774 / 0.3802 — but every variant is ~0.08 below grok, i.e. ~5× the measured ±0.016 judge-noise floor, so "below every system" is robust to noise regardless of which draw you quote. (b) The GPT-5.5 board is small (10 systems), so "below EVERY system" is a weaker statement than it sounds. (c) The secondary "bleeds into insight/comprehensiveness" point is an inference, not something I verified from source — though RACE being reference-based/comparative (SOTA_REVIEW_BRIEF L4) against a reference with 24 H3 / 10 tables / 91 bullets makes it plausible. (d) The "cheapest to fix" judgment is an engineering opinion, not a verifiable fact.
input.corrected: No correction to the verdict. Two precision notes: (1) "Information Presentation" is not a JSON criterion name — it is item 4 of the example <analysis> narrative in criteria_prompt_en.py L448; the actual JSON criteria carrying charts/tables are "Effective Use of Visualizations (Charts, Tables)" (weight 0.10) and "Highlighting of Key Information and Summaries" (0.05). The sentence quoted in the claim is verbatim correct. (2) The claim undersells itself by calling these "exemplar" criteria: the instruction block at criteria_prompt_en.py L413-418 MANDATES that every generated readability rubric cover "Content structure and logic (headings, ...)" and "Data and visualization (... use of charts/tables)", so these are required criteria areas for task 72, not merely few-shot example flavor. Sources: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench/main/prompt/criteria_prompt_en.py (unchanged since 2025-06-13 per GitHub commits API, hence current under the GPT-5.5 evaluator); https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/raw/main/data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv (grok-deeper-search readability 45.72, lowest of 10); https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench/main/prompt/clean_prompt.py L28-29 ("Keep all other original content; remove only citations" — confirms headings/tables/bullets survive the cleaner and reach the judge).
